method0.generated_article:
==Geography==

Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is a volcanic island complex in the Kingdom of Tonga, in the South Pacific Ocean. It lies in the western part of the Tonga island arc, about 65 kilometres (40 mi) north of Tongatapu, the main island of Tonga, and south of the Haʻapai island group. The feature is part of an active submarine volcano whose summit rises above sea level in several small islands and, at times, newly formed volcanic land.

The name commonly refers to the two long-established islands, Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai, and to the temporary landmass that joined them after an eruption in 2014–2015. Hunga Tonga lies to the west and Hunga Haʻapai to the east. Both islands are remnants of the rim of a largely submarine volcanic caldera. Before the 2014–2015 eruption they were separated by open water; after that eruption, a cone of volcanic ash and tuff rose between them and formed a continuous island. The 2022 eruption destroyed most of this new land, leaving only parts of the older islands and submerged volcanic structures.

The islands are uninhabited and have no permanent freshwater sources. Their coastlines are steep, exposed, and difficult to access, with cliffs, narrow beaches, and unstable volcanic slopes. The surrounding waters are deep close to shore because the islands form the emergent summit of a much larger submarine volcano. The shape, size, and elevation of the island complex have changed repeatedly as eruptions, wave erosion, and collapse events have altered the volcanic cone.

==Geology==

Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is part of the Tonga–Kermadec volcanic arc, one of the most active volcanic regions in the world. The volcano is produced by the subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate along the Tonga Trench. This tectonic setting generates frequent earthquakes and volcanism throughout Tonga and neighbouring areas of the southwest Pacific.

The Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai volcano is a large submarine stratovolcano with a summit caldera. Only small portions of the volcano rise above the ocean surface. The pre-existing islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai are interpreted as remnants of the caldera rim. Much of the active volcanic system lies underwater, where eruptions may occur without immediately producing permanent land.

The volcano has produced explosive eruptions involving magma and seawater, a style known as phreatomagmatic or surtseyan activity. Such eruptions fragment magma into ash, lapilli, and volcanic blocks, building low cones of tuff and ash that are highly vulnerable to marine erosion. The 2014–2015 eruption created a new cone and crater lake between the two older islands. The resulting landmass was one of the few modern volcanic islands to survive for several years after a surtseyan eruption, making it an important case study in the formation and erosion of new volcanic islands.

The 2022 eruption was among the most powerful volcanic events recorded in the modern instrumental era. It involved a major explosive eruption from the submarine caldera, generated a high-altitude eruption plume, produced atmospheric pressure waves that travelled around the globe, and created tsunamis across the Pacific. The eruption removed most of the 2014–2015 cone and substantially modified the remaining islands.

==Climate==

Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai has a tropical maritime climate, broadly similar to that of other islands in central and southern Tonga. Temperatures are warm throughout the year, moderated by the surrounding ocean. Seasonal variation is limited, although the months from about November to April are generally warmer, wetter, and more humid.

The island complex is exposed to the southeast trade winds for much of the year. Rainfall is variable and influenced by tropical weather systems, including depressions and cyclones. The cyclone season in Tonga generally runs from November to April, during which strong winds, heavy rain, storm surge, and high waves can affect exposed islands.

Because Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is small, low in parts, and composed largely of unconsolidated volcanic material, it is highly susceptible to erosion by waves, rainfall, and storms. Heavy rainfall can cut gullies into loose ash and tuff, while ocean swell can rapidly remove beaches and undermine cliffs. Climatic and marine processes have therefore played a major role in determining whether newly formed volcanic land persists or disappears.

==Ecology==

The ecology of Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai has been shaped by repeated disturbance, isolation, and the absence of permanent human settlement. The older islands supported limited vegetation before recent eruptions, including coastal grasses, shrubs, and other salt-tolerant plants typical of small Tongan islands. Their vegetation was periodically damaged or destroyed by ashfall, volcanic gases, and explosive activity.

The 2014–2015 volcanic cone initially formed as a barren landscape of ash, tuff, and unstable volcanic debris. Over the following years, it became a natural laboratory for the study of primary ecological succession. Seeds, spores, insects, and microorganisms reached the island by wind, ocean currents, floating debris, and birds. Seabirds played a significant role by nesting or resting on the new land and depositing guano, which enriched the young volcanic soils with nutrients.

Scientists observed the gradual development of microbial communities, early plant colonisation, and erosion-driven changes in habitat. The island was of particular interest because it allowed researchers to compare ecological succession on Earth with processes that may occur on young volcanic terrains elsewhere, including on Mars-like landscapes. The survival of the island for several years made it unusually valuable, as many newly formed surtseyan islands are destroyed within months by wave erosion.

The 2022 eruption greatly altered or eliminated many of the habitats that had developed on the 2014–2015 landmass. Much of the new cone was destroyed, and ashfall and tsunami waves affected the remaining islands. The ecological succession process was therefore largely reset, leaving the remaining terrain subject to renewed colonisation and erosion.

==History==

Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai were known as small, uninhabited volcanic islands before the formation of the temporary land bridge between them. Their isolation, lack of freshwater, steep terrain, and exposure to volcanic hazards prevented permanent settlement. They were nevertheless part of the maritime environment of Tonga and lay within waters used by Tongan navigators, fishers, and later scientific expeditions.

Recorded eruptive activity at the Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai volcano includes events in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries. Eruptions were reported in 1912 and 1937, and further submarine or island-affecting activity occurred later in the century. In 2009, an eruption produced ash plumes and new volcanic deposits near Hunga Haʻapai, damaging vegetation and modifying the coastline.

A major eruption began in December 2014 and continued into January 2015. It produced a new volcanic cone between Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai and joined the two islands into a single landmass. The new island attracted international scientific attention because it was one of the few newly formed volcanic islands in modern times to survive beyond its first few months. Satellite imagery, drone surveys, and field observations documented its rapid erosion, the development of a crater lake, and the beginning of biological colonisation.

Activity resumed in December 2021, leading to a series of eruptions that culminated on 15 January 2022 in a catastrophic explosive event. The eruption generated a massive ash plume, intense lightning, a pressure wave detected worldwide, and tsunamis that affected Tonga and many other Pacific coastlines. In Tonga, ashfall contaminated water supplies, damaged crops, disrupted communications, and affected communities across several islands. The eruption also severed undersea communications cables, temporarily isolating Tonga from much of the outside world.

The 2022 eruption removed most of the land created in 2014–2015 and significantly reshaped Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai. Subsequent satellite observations showed that the island complex had returned to a more fragmented form, with much of the central volcanic cone submerged or destroyed.

==Scientific significance==

Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is scientifically significant because it demonstrates the rapid formation and destruction of volcanic islands in an oceanic arc setting. The 2014–2015 eruption provided a rare opportunity to observe a surtseyan island from birth using modern satellite imagery, aerial surveys, and fieldwork. Researchers studied how volcanic material consolidated, how rainfall and waves eroded the cone, how a crater lake developed, and how plants and animals began to colonise the new land.

The island also became important in planetary science. Its volcanic deposits, erosion patterns, and clay-rich alteration minerals were compared with landforms on Mars, where similar processes may have occurred in the past in the presence of water. Because the island formed quickly and changed rapidly, it offered a natural analogue for studying young volcanic landscapes.

The 2022 eruption made the volcano a major focus of global geophysical research. The event produced an exceptionally high eruption plume, powerful atmospheric waves, widespread tsunami generation, and dramatic caldera modification. It provided new data on submarine caldera eruptions, volcano-generated tsunamis, volcanic lightning, atmospheric coupling, and the hazards posed by island arc volcanoes. The eruption also highlighted the vulnerability of small island states to volcanic hazards, ashfall, tsunamis, and disruption of communications infrastructure.

==Present condition==

Since the 2022 eruption, Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai has consisted mainly of altered remnants of the older islands rather than the continuous landmass created in 2014–2015. The central cone that once connected Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai was largely destroyed, and much of the volcanic structure lies below sea level. The surrounding seafloor and coastline remain subject to ongoing change as loose volcanic deposits are reworked by waves, currents, and gravity-driven collapse.

The volcano remains active and is monitored by Tongan authorities and international scientific organisations using satellite imagery, seismic data, oceanographic observations, and atmospheric measurements. Because much of the volcanic edifice is underwater and the islands are uninhabited, remote sensing is especially important for detecting renewed activity and assessing hazards.

Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai continues to be regarded as a hazardous volcanic area. Future eruptions may create new land, destroy existing remnants, generate ash clouds, or produce tsunamis. Its history illustrates the unstable nature of young volcanic islands and the close connection between geological processes, marine erosion, ecological succession, and natural hazards in the Tonga volcanic arc.

method1.generated_article:
==Geography and volcanic setting==

Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is a volcanic feature near Tonga in the South Pacific Ocean, associated with an underwater volcano whose 2022 eruptive vent was about 200 m below the ocean surface.[3]  
The volcano lies near the Pacific Island nation of Tonga, which was directly affected by the eruption and resulting tsunami.[2]  
Its shallow submarine setting made the 2022 eruption especially important for understanding how underwater volcanic explosions can affect the atmosphere, ocean, and nearby coasts.[3][4]  

==2022 eruption==

On 15 January 2022, Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai erupted explosively, producing the largest underwater explosion recorded by modern scientific instruments.[1]  
The eruption was described by the European Space Agency as the largest eruption of the 21st century and as likely the biggest eruption recorded anywhere on Earth in about 30 years.[2]  
The explosion generated a sonic boom that travelled around the world twice and was heard as far away as Alaska.[2][5]  
Satellite observations showed that the eruption sent volcanic material as high as 58 km into the atmosphere.[2]  
For several hours, the eruption plume produced more lightning over Tonga and nearby waters than the rest of the world combined.[5]  

==Tsunami and local impacts==

The eruption generated a tsunami of nearly 15 m that struck Tonga and destroyed villages.[2]  
The event caused widespread destruction in the Pacific Island nation of Tonga.[2]  
Researchers have described the eruption and tsunami as highly significant for understanding historical and prehistoric tsunamis in the Pacific basin.[4]  
Geologists began emphasizing the need to study Tongan tsunami deposits before they were altered by human activity or natural coastal processes such as tropical storms.[4]  
The eruption also highlighted the importance of studying other South Pacific stratovolcanoes that have received limited previous attention.[4]  

==Atmospheric and geophysical effects==

The eruption injected very large quantities of water vapour into the stratosphere, increasing global stratospheric water vapour by about 10%.[1][5]  
Much of the additional stratospheric water vapour was expected to remain in the atmosphere through 2025.[1]  
The eruption also released a moderate amount of sulfur dioxide, which was converted into sulfate aerosols that reflect sunlight back into space.[3]  
Shockwaves from the eruption travelled through the ground, ocean, and atmosphere.[5]  
One scientific assessment found that the eruption released about an order of magnitude more energy than the 1980 eruption of Mount St Helens.[5]  
Researchers described the event as having “rang the Earth like a bell” because of its global geophysical signals.[5]  

==Climate and ozone effects==

The eruption disturbed Southern Hemisphere stratospheric ozone in the months after the event, but its overall effects on the Antarctic ozone hole were minor.[1]  
Scientific assessments found that the eruption did not cause the record-high global temperatures observed in 2023 and 2024.[1]  
Climate-model simulations indicated that the eruption’s global surface cooling influence was about 0.05 K, a signal indistinguishable from natural climate variability.[1]  
A UCLA-led study concluded that the eruption cooled rather than warmed the Southern Hemisphere, reducing temperatures there by about 0.1 °C.[3]  
The same study found that the global effect from 2022 was a slight cooling, mainly because sulfate aerosols reflected sunlight away from Earth.[3]  
Some evidence suggested a very small warming influence in the Northern Hemisphere, because water vapour can remain in the stratosphere for years.[3]  

==Scientific observation and research==

Satellites were central to observing the eruption, its plume, and its aftermath.[2]  
The European Space Agency used data from its Aeolus mission to create a sonification of the eruption based on global wind observations.[2]  
The eruption became the subject of international scientific collaboration, including the Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai Impact Model Observation Comparison project.[1]  
That project involved more than ten global climate models used to compare observations, data analyses, and simulations of the eruption’s effects.[1]  
Researchers have described the eruption as a natural laboratory for studying underwater shockwaves, tsunamis, atmospheric waves, and coupled ocean–atmosphere processes.[4][5]  
The event motivated new observational and modelling work on how atmospheric pressure disturbances can resonate with ocean waves and amplify tsunami effects.[4]

method2.generated_article:
==Volcano and caldera==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is a mostly submarine volcano in the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, formed by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[4] The volcanic edifice rises more than 1.8 km from the seafloor and is about 20 km across, with only small parts of its summit historically emerging above sea level.[4] Its eruptive products are andesitic, a composition typical of intra-oceanic volcanic arcs.[4]

The summit contains a large caldera, described as about 5 km in diameter, of which Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai were the emergent northern and western remnants.[4] Earlier geological work noted that the two islands were elongated along a circle centred on a rocky shoal south of Hunga Tonga, where eruptions occurred in 1912 and 1937.[2] Samples from Hunga Tonga showed alternating layers of andesitic lava, scoria, lapilli and ash dipping away from the inferred centre, supporting the interpretation that the islands were remnants of a submarine caldera rim.[2] High-resolution multibeam sonar later identified a 4 × 2 km, 150 m deep depression at the expected caldera location, consistent with collapse during an earlier explosive eruption.[2]

An eruption in 2014–2015 built a tephra cone that joined Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai into a single island named Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[4] The new island persisted despite erosion and settling until the major eruption sequence of late 2021 and January 2022.[2] The 15 January 2022 eruption destroyed most of the joined island, leaving only small remnants of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai above the ocean.[4][5] The 2022 event was described as a once-in-1,000-years eruption for the Hunga caldera and may rank as high as VEI 5, although its erupted volume requires further analysis.[5]

==Geography==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is part of the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc in the South Pacific, a volcanic arc formed by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[5]

The volcanic edifice is mostly submarine, rising more than 1.8 km from the seafloor and extending about 20 km across.[5]

Its summit contains a caldera about 5 km in diameter, of which only small portions historically emerged above sea level as the islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai.[5]

The island group lies in Tonga about 65 km north of Tongatapu, the country's main island, and about 30 km south of the submarine volcano Fonuafoʻou.[4]

During the 2014–2015 eruption, tephra accumulated around a new volcanic cone and connected the two previously separate islands, forming the conjoined island of Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[1][4]

The new island was uninhabited and covered just over 1 km2 before the January 2022 eruption, with a maximum elevation of 149 m.[4]

The January 2022 eruption destroyed most of the conjoined island, leaving only very small remnants at the western and eastern edges of the former landmass.[4]

==Eruptions==
On 15 January 2022, at about 5 p.m. local time, the submarine volcano Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai erupted violently in the South Pacific Kingdom of Tonga.[4] The eruption occurred from a vent about 200 m below the ocean surface and injected large amounts of water vapor, along with a moderate amount of sulfur dioxide, into the stratosphere.[1] The eruption produced a vertical plume more than 30 km tall, with overshooting tops above 55 km, a record height in the satellite era.[5] Surface-pressure data indicated an initial explosion energy release of about 10–28 exajoules, probably greater than the 1991 eruption of Mount Pinatubo and possibly comparable to the 1883 eruption of Krakatoa.[5]

The eruption was unusually explosive because seawater interacted with magma at temperatures usually exceeding 1,000 °C, producing rapid steam expansion in a confined volcanic setting.[3] NASA scientist James Garvin described the event as not being a standard Surtseyan eruption, because much larger quantities of water appear to have entered the magma system.[3] Researchers suggested that weakening and partial collapse of the caldera’s northern rim may have allowed large volumes of seawater to rush into a hot underground magma chamber.[3] Some volcanologists informally described the event as an “ultra Surtseyan” eruption.[3]

The volcano forms the upper part of a large underwater edifice that rises about 1.8 km from the seafloor, spans about 20 km, and is topped by a submarine caldera about 5 km in diameter.[3] Before the 2022 eruption, the island represented part of the rim of the Hunga caldera and was the only portion of the volcanic edifice above sea level.[3] After the eruption, all of the new land was gone, along with large parts of the two older islands.[3]

The eruption generated tsunamis that struck Tonga, where waves reached as high as 65 ft above sea level, destroyed hundreds of structures, displaced more than 1,500 people, and caused four deaths.[4] Tsunami waves also affected other Pacific coasts, causing two deaths and an oil spill in Peru and damage in Fiji, Hawaii, Chile, California, Japan, Russia, and New Zealand.[4] Surveys after the eruption found fine sandy mud, deep ash ripples up to 50 km from the volcano, gouged valleys, and large sediment deposits on the seafloor.[4] Tonga’s domestic internet cable was severed during the eruption and was found buried beneath about 100 ft of sediment and ash.[4]

Monitoring the eruption sequence was difficult because there were no seismometers on Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai and the nearest functioning seismic station was nearly 500 mi away.[4] Scientists from the USGS National Earthquake Information Center developed methods to identify earthquakes near the volcano that automated systems had not detected.[4] Working with the USGS Volcano Disaster Assistance Program, they constructed a near-real-time catalogue of earthquakes near the volcano in the days and weeks after the eruption.[4]

The eruption injected an unprecedented amount of water vapor into the stratosphere for a volcanic event, increasing total stratospheric water content by about 10%.[2] It also released sulfur dioxide, which was converted into sulfate aerosols that reflect sunlight back into space.[1] Because water vapor and sulfate aerosols have opposing climate effects, scientists initially debated whether the eruption would warm or cool the planet.[1][2] Later studies found that the eruption produced a slight net cooling effect, including a reduction of about 0.1 °C over the Southern Hemisphere from 2022.[1]

==Footnotes==
The volcano is described in scientific literature as an underwater volcano in the Tonga–Kermadec volcanic arc, located near 20.536°S, 175.382°W and about 150 m below sea level.[1]

The 15 January 2022 eruption has been characterized as one of the largest volcanic events recorded with modern geophysical instruments, with an estimated VEI of about 6 and a plume reported to have reached up to 57 km altitude.[1]

The eruption generated tsunamis affecting Tonga and was reported to have produced tsunami waves across the Pacific, including in Australia, Japan, and the west coasts of North and South America.[2]

One proposed trigger for the exceptional explosivity was a submarine landslide, with comparisons drawn to other eruptions in which slope failure preceded major explosive activity.[2]

Researchers have emphasized that the event has important implications for understanding both historical and prehistoric Pacific tsunamis and for studying coupled atmospheric–oceanographic wave processes.[3]

Subsequent climate studies reported that the eruption injected unusually large amounts of water vapor into the stratosphere, producing contrasting temperature effects at different stratospheric levels.[4]

A UCLA-led analysis reported that the eruption produced net cooling over the Southern Hemisphere of about 0.1 °C by the end of 2022 and 2023, despite earlier expectations that stratospheric water vapor might cause warming.[5]

Data associated with one recent study of the eruption were made available through supplementary files and deposited in the PANGAEA database and Figshare repository.[1]

sections.content:
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is a mostly submarine volcano in the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, formed by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[4] The volcanic edifice rises more than 1.8 km from the seafloor and is about 20 km across, with only small parts of its summit historically emerging above sea level.[4] Its eruptive products are andesitic, a composition typical of intra-oceanic volcanic arcs.[4]

The summit contains a large caldera, described as about 5 km in diameter, of which Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai were the emergent northern and western remnants.[4] Earlier geological work noted that the two islands were elongated along a circle centred on a rocky shoal south of Hunga Tonga, where eruptions occurred in 1912 and 1937.[2] Samples from Hunga Tonga showed alternating layers of andesitic lava, scoria, lapilli and ash dipping away from the inferred centre, supporting the interpretation that the islands were remnants of a submarine caldera rim.[2] High-resolution multibeam sonar later identified a 4 × 2 km, 150 m deep depression at the expected caldera location, consistent with collapse during an earlier explosive eruption.[2]

An eruption in 2014–2015 built a tephra cone that joined Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai into a single island named Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[4] The new island persisted despite erosion and settling until the major eruption sequence of late 2021 and January 2022.[2] The 15 January 2022 eruption destroyed most of the joined island, leaving only small remnants of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai above the ocean.[4][5] The 2022 event was described as a once-in-1,000-years eruption for the Hunga caldera and may rank as high as VEI 5, although its erupted volume requires further analysis.[5]

Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is part of the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc in the South Pacific, a volcanic arc formed by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[5]

The volcanic edifice is mostly submarine, rising more than 1.8 km from the seafloor and extending about 20 km across.[5]

Its summit contains a caldera about 5 km in diameter, of which only small portions historically emerged above sea level as the islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai.[5]

The island group lies in Tonga about 65 km north of Tongatapu, the country's main island, and about 30 km south of the submarine volcano Fonuafoʻou.[4]

During the 2014–2015 eruption, tephra accumulated around a new volcanic cone and connected the two previously separate islands, forming the conjoined island of Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[1][4]

The new island was uninhabited and covered just over 1 km2 before the January 2022 eruption, with a maximum elevation of 149 m.[4]

The January 2022 eruption destroyed most of the conjoined island, leaving only very small remnants at the western and eastern edges of the former landmass.[4]

On 15 January 2022, at about 5 p.m. local time, the submarine volcano Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai erupted violently in the South Pacific Kingdom of Tonga.[4] The eruption occurred from a vent about 200 m below the ocean surface and injected large amounts of water vapor, along with a moderate amount of sulfur dioxide, into the stratosphere.[1] The eruption produced a vertical plume more than 30 km tall, with overshooting tops above 55 km, a record height in the satellite era.[5] Surface-pressure data indicated an initial explosion energy release of about 10–28 exajoules, probably greater than the 1991 eruption of Mount Pinatubo and possibly comparable to the 1883 eruption of Krakatoa.[5]

The eruption was unusually explosive because seawater interacted with magma at temperatures usually exceeding 1,000 °C, producing rapid steam expansion in a confined volcanic setting.[3] NASA scientist James Garvin described the event as not being a standard Surtseyan eruption, because much larger quantities of water appear to have entered the magma system.[3] Researchers suggested that weakening and partial collapse of the caldera’s northern rim may have allowed large volumes of seawater to rush into a hot underground magma chamber.[3] Some volcanologists informally described the event as an “ultra Surtseyan” eruption.[3]

The volcano forms the upper part of a large underwater edifice that rises about 1.8 km from the seafloor, spans about 20 km, and is topped by a submarine caldera about 5 km in diameter.[3] Before the 2022 eruption, the island represented part of the rim of the Hunga caldera and was the only portion of the volcanic edifice above sea level.[3] After the eruption, all of the new land was gone, along with large parts of the two older islands.[3]

The eruption generated tsunamis that struck Tonga, where waves reached as high as 65 ft above sea level, destroyed hundreds of structures, displaced more than 1,500 people, and caused four deaths.[4] Tsunami waves also affected other Pacific coasts, causing two deaths and an oil spill in Peru and damage in Fiji, Hawaii, Chile, California, Japan, Russia, and New Zealand.[4] Surveys after the eruption found fine sandy mud, deep ash ripples up to 50 km from the volcano, gouged valleys, and large sediment deposits on the seafloor.[4] Tonga’s domestic internet cable was severed during the eruption and was found buried beneath about 100 ft of sediment and ash.[4]

Monitoring the eruption sequence was difficult because there were no seismometers on Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai and the nearest functioning seismic station was nearly 500 mi away.[4] Scientists from the USGS National Earthquake Information Center developed methods to identify earthquakes near the volcano that automated systems had not detected.[4] Working with the USGS Volcano Disaster Assistance Program, they constructed a near-real-time catalogue of earthquakes near the volcano in the days and weeks after the eruption.[4]

The eruption injected an unprecedented amount of water vapor into the stratosphere for a volcanic event, increasing total stratospheric water content by about 10%.[2] It also released sulfur dioxide, which was converted into sulfate aerosols that reflect sunlight back into space.[1] Because water vapor and sulfate aerosols have opposing climate effects, scientists initially debated whether the eruption would warm or cool the planet.[1][2] Later studies found that the eruption produced a slight net cooling effect, including a reduction of about 0.1 °C over the Southern Hemisphere from 2022.[1]

The volcano is described in scientific literature as an underwater volcano in the Tonga–Kermadec volcanic arc, located near 20.536°S, 175.382°W and about 150 m below sea level.[1]

The 15 January 2022 eruption has been characterized as one of the largest volcanic events recorded with modern geophysical instruments, with an estimated VEI of about 6 and a plume reported to have reached up to 57 km altitude.[1]

The eruption generated tsunamis affecting Tonga and was reported to have produced tsunami waves across the Pacific, including in Australia, Japan, and the west coasts of North and South America.[2]

One proposed trigger for the exceptional explosivity was a submarine landslide, with comparisons drawn to other eruptions in which slope failure preceded major explosive activity.[2]

Researchers have emphasized that the event has important implications for understanding both historical and prehistoric Pacific tsunamis and for studying coupled atmospheric–oceanographic wave processes.[3]

Subsequent climate studies reported that the eruption injected unusually large amounts of water vapor into the stratosphere, producing contrasting temperature effects at different stratospheric levels.[4]

A UCLA-led analysis reported that the eruption produced net cooling over the Southern Hemisphere of about 0.1 °C by the end of 2022 and 2023, despite earlier expectations that stratospheric water vapor might cause warming.[5]

Data associated with one recent study of the eruption were made available through supplementary files and deposited in the PANGAEA database and Figshare repository.[1]

method3.generated_article:
==Volcano and caldera==
The Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai volcano is a mostly submarine volcano in the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, formed by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[4] The volcanic edifice rises more than 1.8 km from the seafloor and extends about 20 km across, with only small parts of its summit emerging above sea level.[4] Its summit contains a large caldera, described as about 5 km in diameter, of which Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai were the exposed northern and western remnants.[4][2]

Before the 2014–2015 eruption, the site consisted of two small uninhabited islands: Hunga Tonga to the north and Hunga Haʻapai to the west.[2] Geological sampling on Hunga Tonga found alternating layers of andesitic lava flows, scoria, lapilli and ash that dipped gently away from a central area, supporting the interpretation that the islands formed part of a caldera rim.[2] High-resolution multibeam sonar later identified a 4 km by 2 km, 150 m deep depression at the expected caldera location, consistent with collapse during an earlier large explosive eruption.[2]

The volcano’s eruptive products are andesitic, a composition typical of intra-oceanic volcanic arcs.[4] Eruptions were recorded near a rocky shoal about 3 km south of Hunga Tonga in 1912 and 1937, and later activity continued to build and modify the summit area.[2] The 2014–2015 eruption produced a large tephra cone that joined Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai into a single island, commonly called Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[4][5]

The 15 January 2022 eruption was described as a once-in-1,000-years event for the Hunga caldera and may rank as high as VEI 5, although further analysis was needed to confirm the erupted volume.[5] The eruption destroyed most of the 2014–2015 island, leaving only small remnants of the former Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai above the ocean surface.[4][5] Modern observations characterized the 2022 event as the largest underwater explosion recorded by scientific instruments and one of the most energetic natural explosions of the modern geophysical record.[1][3]

==Geography==
The emergent Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai landmass lay in the Tonga archipelago, about 65 km north of Tongatapu, Tonga’s main island, and roughly 30 km south of Fonuafoʻou.[4]  

Before the January 2022 eruption, the joined island covered just over 1 km² and reached a maximum elevation of 149 m above sea level.[4]  

The 2022 eruption removed most of the island’s surface expression, leaving only very small remnants at the western and eastern edges of the former landmass.[4]  

The island’s short-lived central landform attracted geomorphological study because erosion of the new volcanic deposits could be monitored by satellite.[5]  

NASA Earth Observatory noted that study of the island’s changing landforms also had relevance for interpreting similar-looking volcanic and erosional features observed on Mars.[5]  

Ash from the 2022 eruption was deposited across the Haʻapai, Tongatapu, and ʻEua island groups, with reported thicknesses of about 5–50 mm and about 20–30 mm on Tongatapu.[4]  

Tsunami waves generated at the volcano affected inhabited islands in Tonga and propagated across the Pacific to coastlines as far away as South America, North America, and Northeast Asia.[4]

==Eruptions==
The climactic January 2022 eruption produced a vertical plume more than 30 km high, with overshooting tops above 55 km, a height described as a record for the satellite era.[5] Analysis of surface-pressure data estimated the initial explosion released about 10–28 exajoules of energy, probably exceeding the 1991 eruption of Mount Pinatubo and possibly approaching the scale of the 1883 Krakatoa eruption.[5] The event was highly explosive because very hot magma, typically exceeding 1,000 °C, interacted with much cooler seawater in a confined setting.[3] NASA scientist James Garvin described the eruption as unlike a standard Surtseyan eruption, and some volcanologists informally referred to it as an “ultra Surtseyan” event because of the unusually large amount of water involved.[3] One proposed explanation is that part of the northern caldera rim failed, allowing large volumes of seawater to enter an underground magma chamber, although the absence of seismometers on the volcano limited direct confirmation of this process.[3]

The eruption generated tsunamis that reached as high as 65 ft (20 m) above sea level on Tongan islands, destroyed hundreds of structures, displaced more than 1,500 people, and caused four deaths in Tonga.[4] Tsunami waves also crossed the Pacific, causing two deaths and an oil spill in Peru and producing damage or impacts in Fiji, Hawaii, Chile, California, Japan, Russia, and New Zealand.[4] Because the nearest functioning seismic station was nearly 500 mi (800 km) away, the United States Geological Survey developed methods to detect earthquakes that automated systems had missed and compiled a near-real-time catalogue of activity near the volcano after the eruption.[4] Subsequent seafloor surveys found fine sandy mud, deep ash ripples, gouged valleys, and large sediment deposits as far as 50 km from the volcano.[4] Tonga’s domestic internet cable, severed during the eruption, was found buried beneath about 100 ft (30 m) of sediment and ash.[4]

The eruption injected an unusually large amount of water vapor, along with volcanic aerosols, into the stratosphere.[1][2] As a submarine eruption, it increased total stratospheric water content by about 10%, an exceptional perturbation compared with most large eruptions.[2] The eruption also emitted sulfur dioxide, which was converted into sulfate aerosols that reflect sunlight and have a cooling effect, while stratospheric water vapor can exert a warming influence.[1] Later climate analyses found that the eruption did not cause net warming; instead, it produced a slight global cooling effect from 2022, including an estimated 0.1 °C cooling over the Southern Hemisphere.[1] Some studies nevertheless noted possible signs of a small warming influence in the Northern Hemisphere, consistent with the longer residence time of water vapor in the stratosphere.[1]

==Footnotes==
Scientific datasets associated with later studies of the eruption have been archived in PANGAEA and Figshare, with supplementary material made available through the journal publisher.[1]

Early explanatory commentary proposed a submarine landslide as a likely trigger for the unusually explosive phase, noting that removal of overlying rock can destabilize volcanic systems.[2]

Reviews of the event emphasized that tsunami deposits in Tonga required prompt study before disturbance by human activity or coastal processes, and identified coupled atmospheric–oceanographic resonance as a key area for further research.[3]

Subsequent atmospheric studies differed in emphasis: one reported prolonged mid-stratospheric cooling with lower-stratospheric warming, while another found net Southern Hemisphere cooling when water vapor, sulfate aerosols, and ozone effects were considered together.[4][5]
